title: W1 B Set Theory
body:
Mathematical Economics

Juan Carlos Carbajal

School of Economics UNSW–Sydney

Week 1B — Term 1, 2026

1/11  Elements of Set Theory

2/11

Definition 1

A set S is any collection of elements. A set T is a subset of S if every element in T also belongs to S, in which case we write T ⊆ S.

3/11

Definition 1

A set S is any collection of elements. A set T is a subset of S if every element in T also belongs to S, in which case we write T ⊆ S.

◦ Sets can be described in different ways. A = {1, 3, 5, 7, 9, . . .} or equivalently A = {2n − 1 : n ∈ N}; B = {2, 4, 8, 16, 32, . . .} or equivalently B = {2n : n ∈ N}.

◦ We can define a set via a specific property: S ≡ {x : x satisfies P }.

3/11

Definition 1

A set S is any collection of elements. A set T is a subset of S if every element in T also belongs to S, in which case we write T ⊆ S.

◦ Sets can be described in different ways. A = {1, 3, 5, 7, 9, . . .} or equivalently A = {2n − 1 : n ∈ N}; B = {2, 4, 8, 16, 32, . . .} or equivalently B = {2n : n ∈ N}.

◦ We can define a set via a specific property: S ≡ {x : x satisfies P }.

Example 1

In a two-good economy, the competitive budget set B is given by B = {x ∈ R2+ : p1 x1 + p2 x2 ≤ m}.

3/11

◦ If S contains the element x, write x ∈ S.

◦ If x does not belong to S, we write x ∈ / S.

◦ The cardinality of a set is the number of elements it contains.

4/11

◦ If S contains the element x, write x ∈ S.

◦ If x does not belong to S, we write x ∈ / S.

◦ The cardinality of a set is the number of elements it contains.

Lemma 1

Let S and T be two sets. (a) S = T if and only if S ⊆ T and T ⊆ S. (b) If T is finite and S ⊆ T , then |S| ≤ |T |.

4/11

Let B(p1 , p2 , m) denote the budget set given market prices p1 , p2 > 0 and income level m ≥ 0.

We explore how budget sets are affected when prices or income change.

Lemma 2

(a) If p′1 > p1 , then B(p′1 , p2 , m) ⊆ B(p1 , p2 , m).

(b) If m′ > m, then B(p1 , p2 , m) ⊆ B(p1 , p2 , m′ ).

(c) If p′1 > p1 and p′2 < p2 , then B(p′1 , p′2 , m) ̸⊆ B(p1 , p2 , m) and B(p1 , p2 , m) ̸⊆ B(p′1 , p′2 , m).

5/11

◦ Since x ∈ B(p′1 , p2 , m), we know p′1 x1 + p2 x2 ≤ m.

◦ Since p′1 > p1 , we also know that p1 x1 ≤ p′1 x1 (note the weak inequality).

◦ From these two observations it follows that p1 x1 + p2 x2 ≤ m, thus

◦ Since x ∈ B(p′1 , p2 , m), we know p′1 x1 + p2 x2 ≤ m.

◦ Since p′1 > p1 , we also know that p1 x1 ≤ p′1 x1 (note the weak inequality).

◦ From these two observations it follows that p1 x1 + p2 x2 ≤ m, thus

(c) It suffices to find some x′ that belongs to B(p′1 , p′2 , m) but not to B(p1 , p2 , m), and some x that belongs to B(p1 , p2 , m) but not to B(p′1 , p′2 , m).

◦ Since x ∈ B(p′1 , p2 , m), we know p′1 x1 + p2 x2 ≤ m.

◦ Since p′1 > p1 , we also know that p1 x1 ≤ p′1 x1 (note the weak inequality).

◦ From these two observations it follows that p1 x1 + p2 x2 ≤ m, thus

(c) It suffices to find some x′ that belongs to B(p′1 , p′2 , m) but not to B(p1 , p2 , m), and some x that belongs to B(p1 , p2 , m) but not to B(p′1 , p′2 , m). ◦ Take x′ = (x′1 , x′2 ) = (0, m/p′2 ) — i.e., the consumer spends all her money on good 2.

◦ Clearly p′1 x′1 + p′2 x′2 = m, thus x′ ∈ B(p′1 , p′2 , m).

◦ However, since p2 > p′2 , p2 p1 x′1 + p2 x′2 = p′2 m > m.

(b) intersection: S ∩ T ≡ {x : x ∈ S and x ∈ T };

(b) intersection: S ∩ T ≡ {x : x ∈ S and x ∈ T };

(c) product: S × T ≡ {(s, t) : s ∈ S, t ∈ T };

(b) intersection: S ∩ T ≡ {x : x ∈ S and x ∈ T };

(c) product: S × T ≡ {(s, t) : s ∈ S, t ∈ T };

(d) complement: S c = {x : x ∈ / S};

(e) power set: 2S = {A : A ⊆ S}.

(b) A ∩ B = {1} but A ∩ C

2S = {a}, {b}, {c}, {a, b}, {a, c}, {b, c}, {a, b, c}, ∅ .  

Commutative and Associative Laws

Commutative laws: Associative laws:

◦ S ∪ T = T ∪ S. ◦ X ∪ (Y ∪ Z) = (X ∪ Y ) ∪ Z.

◦ S ∩ T = T ∩ S. ◦ X ∩ (Y ∩ Z) = (X ∩ Y ) ∩ Z.

9/11   Further Properties

Exercise 1

For arbitrary sets S and T , the following must be true. (a) S ⊆ (S ∪ T );

(b) (S ∩ T ) ⊆ S;

(c) S ∪ T = (S ∩ T c ) ∪ (T ∩ S c ) ∪ (S ∩ T ).

10/11   De Morgan’s Laws

Proposition 1

Given two sets S and T , one has (S ∪ T )c = S c ∩ T c and (S ∩ T )c = S c ∪ T c .

11/11   De Morgan’s Laws

Proposition 1

Given two sets S and T , one has (S ∪ T )c = S c ∩ T c and (S ∩ T )c = S c ∪ T c .

Proof Let S and T be given. Then (S ∪ T )c = x : x ∈   / (S ∪ T )   = x: x∈ / S and x ∈/T = x : x ∈ S and x ∈ T c c = Sc ∩ T c.  

The other statement is proven similarly.
